notes.note: The saltus Castulonensis, the mountain defile of the Sierra Morena near the town of Castulo, the chief land route between Pollio's Further Spain and the rest of the empire; Pollio explains that brigands and rival patrols have made it impassable to couriers.

notes.anchor: the talk of the man whom, though no one wants to look at him, men nonetheless do not hate as he deserves
notes.note: Pollio leaves the man pointedly unnamed, but he is Lepidus, the neighbouring governor of Narbonese Gaul and Nearer Spain whose couriers Pollio names openly in section 4; the circumlocution guards against the letter being intercepted.

notes.anchor: Caesar, however, for treating me --- newly known to him, in the midst of such fortune --- as one of his oldest intimates
notes.note: The dictator Julius Caesar (murdered March 44 BC), not the young Octavian; Pollio recalls the personal favour Caesar showed him during the civil war while distancing himself from any new "domination."

notes.anchor: That at Corduba I said before the assembly
notes.note: Renders pro contione: a formal public address to a non-voting assembly (contio) of citizens or soldiers, here the provincials and garrison of Corduba, capital of Further Spain.

notes.anchor: what struggles I had about handing over the Thirtieth Legion
notes.note: The Legio XXX, Pollio's most battle-hardened unit; he resisted demands to surrender it precisely because it was the core of his bargaining power as governor of Further Spain.
